_meta.notes: v2.0: 39 diseases + 5 red flags. All original 21 retained with expanded casual/childlike keyword variants. 18 new diseases added covering common gaps. Specialties used: Cardiology, Pediatrics, Orthopedics, Dermatology, General Medicine, Gynecology, Neurology, Psychiatry, ENT, Ophthalmology — all present in seeded doctors table.

disclaimer: I'm an information assistant, not a doctor. The suggestions below are possibilities — never a diagnosis. Please confirm with a qualified physician.

red_flags.label: Possible heart attack
red_flags.advice: Symptoms suggest a possible heart attack. Call emergency services (102 in Nepal) immediately. Do not drive yourself to a hospital.

red_flags.label: Possible stroke
red_flags.advice: Symptoms may indicate a stroke. Call emergency services (102) immediately. Note the time symptoms started.

red_flags.label: Severe allergic reaction
red_flags.advice: Possible anaphylaxis. Call emergency services (102) immediately.

red_flags.label: Severe breathing difficulty
red_flags.advice: Severe trouble breathing requires urgent care. Call 102 or go to the nearest emergency room.

red_flags.label: Suicidal thoughts
red_flags.advice: If you are in crisis, please reach out now. In Nepal, call 1166. Speaking to a professional immediately can help.

diseases.name: Common cold
diseases.description: A mild viral upper-respiratory infection that usually resolves in 7–10 days.
diseases.advice: Rest, drink warm fluids, and use saline nasal drops. See a doctor if symptoms persist beyond 10 days or fever exceeds 39°C.

diseases.name: Influenza (flu)
diseases.description: A more severe viral infection than a cold, with high fever and significant body aches.
diseases.advice: Rest, hydrate, take paracetamol for fever. See a doctor if breathing becomes difficult or fever stays above 39°C for > 3 days.

diseases.name: Migraine
diseases.description: A recurring headache disorder, often one-sided, with nausea and light sensitivity.
diseases.advice: Rest in a dark, quiet room. See a neurologist if migraines occur more than 4 times a month.

diseases.name: Tension headache
diseases.description: Dull pressure or a tight band around the head, often related to stress or poor posture.
diseases.advice: OTC pain relief, hydration, and stress management. Persistent or worsening headaches should be evaluated.

diseases.name: Acid reflux (GERD)
diseases.description: Stomach acid flowing back up, causing burning chest discomfort and a sour taste.
diseases.advice: Avoid spicy/oily foods, eat smaller meals. Antacids help short-term; see a GP if symptoms persist beyond 2 weeks.

diseases.name: Gastroenteritis (stomach flu)
diseases.description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines, usually viral.
diseases.advice: Drink ORS frequently. Eat bland foods. Seek urgent care for blood in stool, dehydration, or symptoms lasting > 3 days.

diseases.name: Urinary tract infection (UTI)
diseases.description: An infection in the urinary system, more common in women.
diseases.advice: Drink water. See a doctor for antibiotics — UTIs rarely clear without treatment.

diseases.name: Hypertension (high blood pressure)
diseases.description: Persistently elevated blood pressure, often without obvious symptoms.
diseases.advice: Have BP checked. Reduce salt, exercise regularly. A cardiologist or GP can advise on medication.

diseases.name: Asthma
diseases.description: A chronic airway condition causing wheezing, breathlessness and chest tightness.
diseases.advice: Use a prescribed inhaler. Avoid known triggers. Severe attacks need emergency care.

diseases.name: Eczema (atopic dermatitis)
diseases.description: Chronic inflammatory skin condition with itchy, dry, red patches.
diseases.advice: Moisturise frequently. Avoid harsh soaps. See a dermatologist if severe.

diseases.name: Acne
diseases.description: Pimples, blackheads, and inflamed spots from clogged hair follicles.
diseases.advice: Wash gently twice a day. Don't pick. Persistent acne benefits from dermatologist treatment.

diseases.name: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever)
diseases.description: Allergic reaction to airborne triggers causing sneezing and nasal symptoms.
diseases.advice: Antihistamines help most cases. See an ENT if persistent.

diseases.name: Sinusitis
diseases.description: Inflammation of the sinuses, often following a cold.
diseases.advice: Steam inhalation, saline rinses, and rest. See an ENT if symptoms last over 10 days.

diseases.name: Conjunctivitis (pink eye)
diseases.description: Inflammation of the eye membrane, usually viral or bacterial. Highly contagious.
diseases.advice: Don't touch the eye. Wash hands frequently. See an eye doctor if it lasts more than a few days.

diseases.name: Sprain or strain
diseases.description: Stretched or torn ligaments or muscles from a fall, twist, or overuse.
diseases.advice: RICE — Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. See a doctor if severe.

diseases.name: Lower back pain
diseases.description: Mechanical pain in the lower back, often from posture or lifting.
diseases.advice: Avoid extended bed rest. Apply heat. See a doctor if pain radiates down the leg.

diseases.name: Anxiety
diseases.description: Persistent worry, restlessness, or fear that interferes with daily life.
diseases.advice: Talk therapy and lifestyle changes help most cases. A psychiatrist can advise if severe.

diseases.name: Depression
diseases.description: Persistent sadness and loss of interest, lasting more than two weeks.
diseases.advice: Reach out to a psychiatrist or counsellor. If you have thoughts of self-harm, call 1166 immediately.

diseases.name: Childhood fever
diseases.description: Fever in children, often viral.
diseases.advice: Hydrate, use paracetamol per package weight-dose. Seek urgent care if the child is < 3 months, fever > 39°C, or lethargic.

diseases.name: Pregnancy-related symptoms
diseases.description: Common pregnancy symptoms include morning sickness, fatigue, and missed periods.
diseases.advice: Take a home pregnancy test. Book an antenatal visit with a gynecologist.

diseases.name: Diabetes (suspected)
diseases.description: Persistently high blood sugar, with thirst, frequent urination, and fatigue.
diseases.advice: Get a fasting blood sugar test. A GP will advise on diet and medication.

diseases.name: Toothache
diseases.description: Tooth pain is most often caused by cavities, gum disease, or a cracked tooth. A dentist's evaluation is needed (your provider list doesn't include one yet — see a GP for triage).
diseases.advice: Rinse with warm salt water; avoid very hot or cold foods. See a dentist as soon as possible. A GP can prescribe pain relief in the meantime.

diseases.name: Food poisoning
diseases.description: Illness caused by contaminated food, usually with sudden vomiting and diarrhoea within hours of eating.
diseases.advice: Sip ORS or clear fluids slowly, rest. See a doctor if symptoms last > 48 hours, you're severely dehydrated, or there's blood in vomit/stool.

diseases.name: Dehydration
diseases.description: Insufficient body fluid, often from heat, vomiting, diarrhoea, or not drinking enough water.
diseases.advice: Sip ORS or water with a pinch of salt and sugar. Severe cases (sunken eyes, very little urine, confusion) need urgent IV fluids.

diseases.name: Chronic fatigue (suspected)
diseases.description: Persistent tiredness not relieved by rest. Could indicate anaemia, thyroid issues, depression, sleep disorders, or other underlying causes.
diseases.advice: See a GP for blood tests (CBC, thyroid, vitamin D, B12). If sleep is the issue, also consider a sleep evaluation.

diseases.name: Vertigo (BPPV)
diseases.description: A sudden sense of spinning, often triggered by head movement.
diseases.advice: Most cases settle with simple positioning manoeuvres. See a neurologist or ENT if recurrent or with hearing changes.

diseases.name: Ear infection
diseases.description: An infection of the middle or outer ear, common in children. Often follows a cold.
diseases.advice: See a doctor — most need antibiotics. Persistent ear pain in young children needs prompt evaluation.

diseases.name: Dry skin (xerosis)
diseases.description: Patches of rough, flaky, sometimes itchy skin — most common in winter or in air-conditioned rooms.
diseases.advice: Use a thick fragrance-free moisturiser twice a day, especially after bathing. Avoid hot showers. See a dermatologist if it doesn't improve in 2–3 weeks.

diseases.name: Dandruff
diseases.description: Scaly, flaky scalp — often a mild form of seborrhoeic dermatitis.
diseases.advice: Use an anti-dandruff shampoo (with zinc pyrithione, ketoconazole, or salicylic acid) 2–3 times a week. Persistent dandruff may need a dermatologist.

diseases.name: Athlete's foot
diseases.description: A common fungal infection between the toes causing itching, peeling, and burning.
diseases.advice: Keep feet dry, change socks daily, and use an OTC antifungal cream. See a dermatologist if it doesn't clear in 2 weeks.

diseases.name: Hemorrhoids
diseases.description: Swollen veins around the anus or lower rectum, causing discomfort, itching, or bleeding during bowel movements.
diseases.advice: Increase fibre and water; avoid straining. Sitz baths and OTC creams help. See a doctor if there's persistent bleeding or severe pain.

keywords: bleeding when going to the toilet

diseases.name: Varicose veins
diseases.description: Enlarged, twisted veins, most often in the legs — caused by weak valves in the veins.
diseases.advice: Elevate legs, wear compression stockings, avoid long periods of standing. See a doctor if veins become painful or skin changes appear.

diseases.name: Motion sickness
diseases.description: Nausea and dizziness from travel — car, bus, boat, plane.
diseases.advice: Sit facing forward, look at the horizon, avoid heavy meals before travel. OTC antihistamines (e.g. dimenhydrinate) taken before travel help most cases.

diseases.name: Hangover
diseases.description: The unpleasant after-effects of drinking too much alcohol — usually dehydration, headache, and nausea.
diseases.advice: Drink water and eat something bland. Rest. Avoid 'hair of the dog'. If you regularly need to drink to feel normal, please speak with a doctor — that's a more serious concern than the hangover itself.

diseases.name: Period cramps (dysmenorrhea)
diseases.description: Cramping pain in the lower abdomen before or during menstruation.
diseases.advice: Heating pads, gentle exercise, and OTC pain relievers help most cases. See a gynecologist if pain is severe enough to disrupt daily life.

diseases.name: Insomnia
diseases.description: Difficulty falling or staying asleep, leading to daytime tiredness.
diseases.advice: Keep a regular sleep schedule, limit screens before bed, avoid afternoon caffeine. A psychiatrist or GP can help if it's chronic.

diseases.name: Attention difficulties (possible ADHD)
diseases.description: Persistent trouble focusing, finishing tasks, or sitting still — could indicate ADHD or another condition. Diagnosis requires a proper psychiatric assessment, not a self-test.
diseases.advice: Please see a psychiatrist for proper evaluation — ADHD is genuinely diagnosable but it isn't something a chatbot can confirm. Bring specific examples of how attention issues affect work, school, or relationships.

diseases.name: Panic attack
diseases.description: A sudden surge of intense fear with physical symptoms — racing heart, sweating, chest tightness, sense of doom — peaking within 10 minutes.
diseases.advice: Slow your breathing (in for 4, hold for 4, out for 4). Find a quiet place. Recurring panic attacks are very treatable — please see a psychiatrist.

keywords: feel like i'm going to die

diseases.name: Social anxiety
diseases.description: Intense fear of social situations, judgement, or embarrassment — strong enough to interfere with daily life.
diseases.advice: Talk therapy (especially CBT) is highly effective. A psychiatrist or counsellor can help build a treatment plan, sometimes with medication.

diseases.name: Minor everyday health concern
diseases.description: Your description sounds like a routine, non-specific complaint. A GP visit is a sensible first step — they can examine you and refer to a specialist if needed.
diseases.advice: Try to describe the symptom more specifically (where, how long, how it feels). Meanwhile, a General Medicine doctor is the right entry point for unclear concerns.
